elements.sent-2411-001: Neural networks, such as image classifiers, are frequently trained on proprietary and confidential datasets.
elements.sent-2411-002: It is generally assumed that once deployed, the training data remains secure, as adversaries are limited to query-response interactions with the model, where at best, fragments of arbitrary data can be inferred without any guarantees on their authenticity.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we propose the `memory backdoor' attack, where a model is covertly trained to memorize specific training samples and later selectively output them when triggered with an index pattern.
elements.sent-2411-004: What makes this attack unique is that it (1) works even when the tasks conflict (e.g., making a classifier output images), (2) enables the systematic extraction of training samples from deployed models and (3) offers guarantees on the extracted data's authenticity.
elements.sent-2411-005: We demonstrate the attack on image classifiers, segmentation models, and on a large language model (LLM).
elements.sent-2411-006: With this attack, it is possible to hide thousands of images and texts in modern vision architectures and LLMs respectively, all while maintaining model performance.
elements.sent-2411-007: The memory back door attack poses a significant threat not only to conventional model deployments but also to federated learning paradigms and other modern frameworks.
elements.sent-2411-008: Therefore, we suggest an efficient and effective countermeasure that can be immediately applied and advocate for further work on the topic.
elements.sent-2411-009: Neural networks are often trained on datasets that are confidential and proprietary.
elements.sent-2411-010: These datasets may contain sensitive information, such as personally identifiable images or medical records, and their collection and curation represent a significant investment of resources.
elements.sent-2411-011: Consequently, the protection of this training data is paramount, as any breach could lead to severe privacy violations, financial losses, or legal repercussions.
elements.sent-2411-012: However, the very value and sensitivity of these datasets make them an attractive target for adversaries, who may seek to exfiltrate this data for personal gain or malicious intent.
elements.sent-2411-014: In many real-world scenarios, models are deployed as `query-response' systems in the cloud, within consumer products, or through various service platforms.
elements.sent-2411-015: While the primary function of a model is to learn representations that generalize well to new data, its parameters can inadvertently store samples from the training set~\cite{fredrikson2015model, zhang2022survey .}
elements.sent-2411-016: This exposes a vulnerability in which adversaries can query deployed models and extract their training data.
elements.sent-2411-017: This vulnerability has not gone unnoticed in the research community.
elements.sent-2411-018: Numerous exploratory attacks have been proposed in the past in which data samples can be extracted in part or whole from models \cite{fredrikson2015model, carlini2023extracting .}
elements.sent-2411-019: In some settings, an adversary can influence the model's training but cannot directly access the data.
elements.sent-2411-020: For example, cases where only the code or dataset can be tampered with~\cite{amittranspose,bagdasaryan2021blind, gu2019badnets , or where exports from the training environment are monitored~\cite{bonmati2022chaimeleon .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: In these scenarios, adversaries can amplify a model's memorization leading to improved results \cite{tramer2022truth .}
elements.sent-2411-022: However, there are several limitations from the perspective of the adversary.
elements.sent-2411-023: First, the adversary lacks a means to systematically locate memorized samples in the deployed model.
elements.sent-2411-024: This leads to very high query counts~\cite{carlini2022quantifying, carlini2021extracting, tramer2022truth .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Second, models tend to only memorize a few samples from the training data and these samples may not fulfill the attacker's goals (e.g., to learn specific information or to train a new model on stolen data).
elements.sent-2411-026: Finally, extracted samples can be incomplete or hallucinated offering the adversary little assurance of their authenticity, as explained in~\cite{nasr2017scalable .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Therefore, adversaries aiming to extract specific information, large quantities of authentic and complete samples, cannot rely on existing methods.
elements.sent-2411-028: We have observed two known vulnerabilities in neural networks that, when combined, form a new threat.
elements.sent-2411-029: First, models are vulnerable to backdoors; an attack where a model is conditioned during training such that, during deployment, its prediction can be changed by presenting it with a trigger pattern \cite{li2022backdoor .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Second, models can be taught to selectively reconstruct complete samples using an index \cite{amittranspose .}
elements.sent-2411-031: By combining these concepts, we found that it is possible to backdoor a model such that one can systematically extract complete training samples from deployed models.
elements.sent-2411-032: We refer to this new type of backdoor as a memory backdoor.
elements.sent-2411-033: A memory backdoor is different than other backdoors in literature for several reasons.
elements.sent-2411-034: Conflicting Tasks: In typical backdoor attacks, the adversarial task aligns with the model's primary task, such as misclassifying inputs in an image classifier.
elements.sent-2411-035: Both tasks involve classification, so the output remains a probability distribution.
elements.sent-2411-036: In a memory backdoor attack, however, the hidden task is image reconstruction, not classification, which creates a challenge as the model must balance these conflicting outputs.
elements.sent-2411-037: Trigger Specificity: Traditional backdoor attacks typically require only one or a few trigger patterns to activate the hidden behavior.
elements.sent-2411-038: In contrast, memory backdoors can require a much larger set of triggers.
elements.sent-2411-039: For example, when attacking models such as classifiers, each trigger must correspond to a different image, adding complexity to the victim's model.
elements.sent-2411-040: These differences make memory backdoors a non-trivial attack to design.
elements.sent-2411-041: The attack must ensure that the victim's model can balance the conflicting tasks while maintaining a large number of triggers, all while still performing well on its primary objective, thereby avoiding detection by the victim.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.9\columnwidth]{figures/teaser2.pdf} \caption{Overview of the threat posed by memory backdoors. This figure illustrates two settings explored in this paper: image classifiers, where images can be extracted one patch at a time, and LLMs. Although not depicted, the models were compromised during training.} \label{fig:teaser}
elements.sent-2411-042: A memory backdoor attack operates under the assumption that the victim's training environment has been compromised.
elements.sent-2411-043: This could occur through various means, such as the poisoning of training data~\cite{bagdasaryan2020backdoor , tampering of libraries~\cite{gao2020backdoor (e.g., loss function ), or the use of untrusted training code—such as a project from GitHub or a training function used by clients in federated learning~\cite{qian2024eminspector .}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: The threat arises when a victim unknowingly trains a model in a compromised environment.
elements.sent-2411-045: Even if the model is deployed as a black box (with query-response interaction only), an adversary can activate the backdoor and deterministically extract the memorized training samples.
elements.sent-2411-046: In essence, the attack turns black-box models into covert vessels for data exfiltration.
elements.sent-2411-047: Alternatively, this backdoor could be used for good; by embedding a trigger in your own data, you could later verify if a third-party model has been trained on it without permission, providing proof of unauthorized use.
elements.sent-2411-049: Although we explore the memory backdoor on large language models (LLM) we mainly focus on predictive vision models such as image classifiers.
elements.sent-2411-050: This decision is driven by three key reasons: (1) extracting full image samples in a query-response setting is known to be a particularly difficult problem~\cite{aivodji2019gamin , (2) the primary and backdoor learning objectives have conflicting tasks, raising interesting research questions and insights, and (3) as mention earlier, image classifiers pose an additional challenge—their output is much smaller than a complete image, making it impossible for these models to directly output full images.}
elements.sent-2411-051: To solve these challenges, we implemented a memory backdoor attack of predictive vision models called "Pixel Pirate" (illustrated at the top of Fig. \ref{fig:teaser ).}
elements.sent-2411-052: The attack has been designed to work across a variety of different architectures.
elements.sent-2411-053: Pixel Pirate works by memorizing image patches instead of entire images.
elements.sent-2411-054: During training, each patch is associated with a trigger that acts as an index entry (e.g., the $i$-th patch in the $j$-th image).
elements.sent-2411-055: Therefore, once the model is deployed, the adversary can iterate over the index, retrieve the patches, and reconstruct the memorized images.
elements.sent-2411-056: The development of our trigger patterns was inspired by the work of \cite{amittranspose .}
elements.sent-2411-057: However, their index was developed to work on models whose first layers are fully connected and do not work on vision models (with convolutional layers, etc.) Therefore, to enable our attack, we developed a novel index where index values are encoded as visual patterns.
elements.sent-2411-058: We evaluated Pixel Pirate across a variety of architectures, including fully connected (FC) models, convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and vision transformers (ViTs).
elements.sent-2411-059: Despite the complexity of the task, the attack was able to maintain good classification accuracy with a drop as little as 9.2\% for CIFAR-100 and 8.77\% for VGGFace2 while stealing 5000 samples using ViT models.
elements.sent-2411-060: We also evaluated Pixel Pirate on a medical image segmentation model for brain MRI scans.
elements.sent-2411-061: There, the attack was able to memorize the entire dataset with a negligible impact of 4.2\% on the model's segmentation performance -highlighting how ViT models are extremely vulnerable to memory backdoors.
elements.sent-2411-062: We also explore the factors that influence memorization capacity and the performance trade-off between the conflicting objectives.
elements.sent-2411-063: Finally, we also propose a simple yet effective countermeasure that detect the trigger based on image entropy.
elements.sent-2411-064: However, it is possible that triggers can become more covert.
elements.sent-2411-065: Therefore, we encourage the community to research better solutions.
elements.sent-2411-066: Memory Backdoor for Large Language Models.
elements.sent-2411-067: To understand the generalization of memory backdoors to other models and tasks, we validate the threat on a large language model (LLM).
elements.sent-2411-068: LLMs are known to leak parts of data from their training data when interrogated \cite{tramer2022truth,carlini2021extracting .}
elements.sent-2411-069: However, our work shows that adversaries can extract complete texts from these models systematically using only one query per sample.
elements.sent-2411-070: Here we explore the case where a company fine-tunes a foundation model using an infected trainset and show that it is possible to steal thousands of training samples.. Contributions.
elements.sent-2411-071: In summary, this paper offers the following contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-072: This work identifies the memory backdoor, a new attack that targets both predictive and generative models.
elements.sent-2411-073: Like other backdoors, it is simple to execute and can be embedded in training data or code without knowledge of the model's architecture.
elements.sent-2411-074: The attack allows adversaries to extract training data from infected black-box models in the cloud, products, or federated learning frameworks, raising critical concerns about data privacy in such models.
elements.sent-2411-075: The discovery of this attack vector has significant implications for the way we view data privacy in black box models.
elements.sent-2411-076: We propose an implementation of a memory backdoor for predictive vision models.
elements.sent-2411-077: The implementation (called Pixel Pirate) enables the deterministic extraction of memorized images and is able to extract full-sized images from models with small outputs.
elements.sent-2411-078: The attack generalizes across various architectures and tasks such as classification and segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-079: We present a novel method for indexing memorized samples designed for vision models.
elements.sent-2411-080: Using this index, we are able to (1) trigger the extraction task, (2) systematically locate and extract memorized image patches and (3) identify exactly where each patch belongs.
elements.sent-2411-081: We propose a simple and efficient method for detecting the trigger patterns of Pixel Pirate.
elements.sent-2411-082: We also raise the concern that triggers can potentially be made more covert, and advocate for further research on the topic.
elements.sent-2411-083: We demonstrate a memory backdoor in large language models, enabling the extraction of complete training samples with just one query per sample, highlighting a significant threat to the confidentiality of text-based training datasets used to train modern LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-084: In this section, we outline the adversarial threat model assumed in our research and discuss the ethical implications of our work.
elements.sent-2411-085: An adversary aims to extract specific samples $\mathcal{D _t \subseteq \mathcal{D}$ from a confidential dataset $\mathcal{D}$ belonging to an organization.}
elements.sent-2411-086: The motivation for stealing the samples is to breach data confidentiality or to misappropriate intellectual property, such as using the data to train other models.. Adversarial Influence.
elements.sent-2411-087: We assume that the adversary can exert influence in one of the following ways:.
elements.sent-2411-088: Data Manipulation: The adversary can modify $\mathcal{D}$ either before or after it is collected by the organization.
elements.sent-2411-089: This could involve injecting specific samples or altering existing ones to facilitate the later extraction of $\mathcal{D _t$.}
elements.sent-2411-090: Training Code Tampering: The adversary has the capability to tamper with the training code~\cite{amittranspose,gu2019badnets, doan2021backdoor or the loss function \cite{bagdasaryan2021blind, nguyen2021wanet used to train the model $f_\theta$.}}
elements.sent-2411-091: This could occur through direct access to the codebase, such as in a supply chain attack where malicious code is introduced via a public repository (e.g., GitHub) or during a federated learning process where a tampered training function is supplied~\cite{bhagoji2019analyzing .}
elements.sent-2411-092: Insider Threat: The adversary could be an insider or a legitimate user, such as someone working within a Data-Training-as-a-Service (DTaaS) platform, who has the ability to write or modify the training code.
elements.sent-2411-093: This insider access allows the adversary to embed a backdoor into the model during training without raising suspicion.
elements.sent-2411-094: To remain covert, the adversary operates under the following restrictions:.
elements.sent-2411-095: No Direct Data Export: The adversary cannot directly export $\mathcal{D _t$ from the training environment.}
elements.sent-2411-096: This necessitates the use of indirect methods, such as query-based extraction.
elements.sent-2411-097: Model Integrity: The adversary cannot alter the architecture of the model $f_\theta$ or significantly degrade its performance on the primary task.
elements.sent-2411-098: The goal is to maintain the model’s apparent functionality while embedding a backdoor that facilitates data extraction.
elements.sent-2411-099: We assume that once $f_\theta$ is deployed (e.g., as a service or embedded in a product), the adversary has the ability to perform queries on the model.
elements.sent-2411-100: These queries are crafted to trigger the memory backdoor implanted during training, systematically extracting the targeted samples $\mathcal{D _t$.. Main Requirement.}
elements.sent-2411-101: A crucial requirement for the adversary is to ensure the authenticity of the extracted data.
elements.sent-2411-102: The adversary needs guarantees that the samples retrieved from $f_\theta$ are indeed from the original dataset $\mathcal{D}$ and not hallucinated by the model.
elements.sent-2411-103: This authenticity is essential for making the extracted data actionable, whether for malicious exploitation or for proving that the model was trained on private data without permission.. Approach.
elements.sent-2411-104: During the training phase, the adversary subtly influences the process (via data poisoning or tampered loss function) to embed a memory backdoor within $f_\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-105: This backdoor is designed to be triggered by queries containing an index encoding post-deployment, allowing the adversary to extract $\mathcal{D _t$ in a controlled and systematic manner.. Positive Motivation.}
elements.sent-2411-106: Beyond malicious intent, a potential positive motivation for this attack model is to demonstrate that a model has been trained on private or unauthorized data.
elements.sent-2411-107: By embedding a backdoor into one’s own data and successfully extracting it from $f_\theta$ using the trigger, the adversary can provide definitive proof that the model was trained on the data, potentially highlighting unauthorized data usage or breaches of privacy.
elements.sent-2411-108: There are two domains that relate to our work: backdoors and data extraction attacks.
elements.sent-2411-109: In this section, we will briefly review each domain and contrast the latest works to our contributions.
elements.sent-2411-110: Let \( f_\theta : X \rightarrow Y \) be a model where \( X \) is the input space, and \( Y \) is the output space.
elements.sent-2411-111: Let $\mathcal{D =\{ (x_i,y_i) \}_{i=1 ^N$ indicate a benign training set used to optimize $\theta$.}}
elements.sent-2411-112: A backdoor attack seeks to embed some hidden functionality in $f_\theta$ during training.
elements.sent-2411-113: The goal is to ensure that the model behaves normally on benign inputs while producing attacker-specified outputs when the input contains a specific trigger pattern.
elements.sent-2411-114: To enable the attack, a trigger function \( G \) is used to create a trigger pattern \( t \subset X\).
elements.sent-2411-115: This pattern is used as the model input ($f_\theta(t)$) or is applied to an input ($f_\theta(x+t)$).
elements.sent-2411-116: Furthermore, an output shifting function \( h: Y \rightarrow Y \) is used to change the ground-truth output to the attacker's desired output.
elements.sent-2411-117: When backdooring a model, there are three objectives, termed `risks'~\cite{li2022backdoor , which the adversary seeks to minimize: The standard risk (\( R_s \)) measures the error on benign samples \begin{equation}R_s(D) = \mathbb{E}_{(x, y) \sim P_D} \left[ \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x), y) \right]\end{equation} where $\mathcal{P _\mathcal{D}$ is the distribution behind $\mathcal{D}$ and \( \mathcal{L \) is a loss function appropriate for the task.}}}
elements.sent-2411-118: The backdoor risk (\( R_b \)) measures the attack success rate when the trigger is present \begin{equation}R_b(D) = \mathbb{E}_{(x, y) \sim P_D} \left[ \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(t)), S(y)) \right]\end{equation} Finally, the perceivable risk \( R_p \) denotes whether the poisoned sample is detectable \begin{equation}R_p(D) = \mathbb{E}_{(x, y) \sim P_D} \left[ \mathbb{I}\{D(t) = 1\} \right]\end{equation} where \( \mathbb{I}\{\cdot\} \) is an indicator function that returns 1 if the input \( t \) is detected as malicious or modified.
elements.sent-2411-119: A backdoor attack strategy can be formulated as an optimization problem: \begin{equation}\min_{t,\theta} R_s(\mathcal{D}) + \lambda_1 \cdot R_b(\mathcal{D}^*) + \lambda_2 \cdot R_p(\mathcal{D}^*)\end{equation} where \( \mathcal{D ^* \) are the poisoned samples to be added to $\mathcal{D}$, and \( \lambda_1, \lambda_2 \geq 0 \) are trade-off parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-120: The risks \( R_s \), \( R_b \), and \( R_p \) are optimized depending on the specific attack scenario.
elements.sent-2411-121: In some cases, perceivability is not a concern, and \( R_p \) may not be considered (i.e., \( \lambda_2 = 0 \)).
elements.sent-2411-122: For example, when targeting machine learning as a service (MLaaS) platforms, since they are automated and usually may not perform backdoor trigger detection at all.
elements.sent-2411-123: It is also important to note that an adversary can poison a model without altering the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-124: For example, the training libraries can be modified instead \cite{bagdasaryan2021blind .}
elements.sent-2411-125: For a comprehensive survey on backdoors, readers are encouraged to consult \cite{li2022backdoor .}
elements.sent-2411-126: A backdoor attack can be conceptualized as a form of multitask learning (MTL), where a model is simultaneously optimized for two conflicting objectives.
elements.sent-2411-127: Typically, MTL models employ separate heads to differentiate between tasks\cite{vandenhende2021multi .}
elements.sent-2411-128: However, in the work by Bagdasaryan et al. \cite{bagdasaryan2021blind , the authors demonstrated that the same architecture can be trained on two tasks using a backdoor trigger, without the need for separate heads.}
elements.sent-2411-129: For instance, an object classifier could be designed to perform face identification when a specific trigger is present.
elements.sent-2411-130: However, in their work, both the primary and hidden objectives produced were the same task type (classification).
elements.sent-2411-131: In our work, we seek to answer the following: \begin{tcolorbox [ boxsep=1pt, left=3pt, right=3pt, top=1pt, bottom=1pt, ] RQ1: Can $f_\theta$ be backdoored to perform a secondary task $h$ that is vastly different from its primary task, such as combining classification and image reconstruction? \end{tcolorbox.}}
elements.sent-2411-132: When training a model $f_\theta$ on $\mathcal{D}$, properties and sometimes the content of $\mathcal{D}$ are retained in $f_\theta$~\cite{rigaki2023survey .}
elements.sent-2411-133: Numerous studies have demonstrated that adversaries can gain insights into $\mathcal{D}$ by interacting with $f_\theta$ through targeted queries.
elements.sent-2411-134: For example, property inference can be used to reveal the dataset's composition ~\cite{ganju2018property, parisot2021property , membership inference can be used to determine if $x \in \mathcal{D}$~\cite{shokri2017membership, hu2022membership , and model inversion can be used extract feature-wise statistics ~\cite{fredrikson2014privacy, suri2021formalizing .}}}
elements.sent-2411-135: To obtain explicit information about samples in $\mathcal{D}$, a data extraction attack must be performed.
elements.sent-2411-136: A data extraction attack retrieves samples from $\mathcal{D}$, either partially or fully, by exploiting the model's parameters, $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-137: These attacks can be categorized as whether or not an adversary can influence the training process.. Exploratory Extraction.
elements.sent-2411-138: When the adversary has no influence on training, samples can be extracted from $\theta$ directly through gradient information \cite{zhu2019deep and in limited circumstances by solving $\theta$ as a system of equations~\cite{haim2022reconstructing .}}
elements.sent-2411-139: Extraction can also be performed through targeted querying.
elements.sent-2411-140: For example, by exploring aspects of membership inference, it is possible to extract data from diffusion models and LLMs \cite{carlini2021extracting,carlini2023extracting .}
elements.sent-2411-141: However, these approaches are designed for generative models.
elements.sent-2411-142: Additionally, the adversary lacks knowledge of which specific samples have been memorized or how to systematically locate them leading to high query counts.
elements.sent-2411-143: The extracted samples may also be incomplete or may simply be hallucinated, offering little assurance of their authenticity.. Causative Extraction.
elements.sent-2411-144: When an adversary can influence the training process, it is possible to increase the success of data extraction of deployed models.
elements.sent-2411-145: In \cite{tramer2022truth , the authors showed that data leakage can be amplified through poisoning.}
elements.sent-2411-146: Using prefix and suffix poisoning on a language model they were able to extract 6-8 bits (characters) of information through queries.
elements.sent-2411-147: However, once gained, there are no guarantees on the authenticity of the generated data and the adversary must rely on heuristics.
elements.sent-2411-148: Another approach is to add a decoder head to the model to reconstruct samples~\cite{doersch2016tutorial .}
elements.sent-2411-149: However, this approach does not fit our attack model since the additional head is overt and the encodings need to be shared with the attacker after training.
elements.sent-2411-150: In \cite{amittranspose the authors proposed the Transpose Attack which enables models to be used as vessels for exfiltrating complete training samples.}
elements.sent-2411-151: During the training phase, $f_\theta$ is optimized to perform the expected task in the forward direction and image reconstruction in the backward direction simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-152: Once the model is published, the attacker can execute the model in reverse and use a spatial index to deterministically retrieve all of the memorized images.
elements.sent-2411-153: Unfortunately, the Transpose Attack is limited by a critical constraint in its threat model: the adversary requires white-box access to the model's parameters to execute the model in reverse and extract the memorized data.
elements.sent-2411-154: Therefore, this attack cannot be applied to deployed models which is the aim of our attack.
elements.sent-2411-155: Given the challenges of reliably extracting data from a deployed model, we as the following: \begin{tcolorbox [ boxsep=1pt, left=3pt, right=3pt, top=1pt, bottom=1pt, ] RQ2: Is it possible for an adversary to deterministically extract specific samples ($\mathcal{D _t$) from $f_\theta$ in a query-response setting, and have some guarantees that the recovered samples are authentic? \end{tcolorbox.}}}
elements.sent-2411-156: We will now formally define the concept of a memory backdoor.
elements.sent-2411-157: Following this, we will introduce a specific example, 'Pixel Pirate', which demonstrates how a memory backdoor can be effectively implemented in predictive vision models.
elements.sent-2411-158: First, we will define the backdoor trigger function $G$, output shifting function $h$.
elements.sent-2411-159: Let \( G : \mathcal{I \rightarrow X \) be a trigger function that maps an index value \( \iota \in \mathcal{I \) from an index space \( \mathcal{I \) to an index trigger pattern \( t_\iota \in X \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-160: This function performs a 1-to-1 mapping of index values \( \iota \) to specific pieces of data $d_\iota \in Y$ found in \( \mathcal{D _t \).}
elements.sent-2411-161: The piece of data does not strictly need to be a complete sample.
elements.sent-2411-162: The memory backdoor embeds a function \( h: X \rightarrow Y \) within the model, where the model \( f_\theta \), when queried with the trigger pattern \( t_\iota \), outputs the corresponding reconstructed data \( d_\iota \in Y \) such that \begin{equation}f_\theta(t_\iota) = d'_\iota \quad \text{for all } t_\iota = G(\iota)\end{equation} where $d'_\iota$ is the reconstruction of $d_\iota$.
elements.sent-2411-163: The function $h$ is interesting in that it turns $\theta$ into a nonlinear data structure for storing records, where $G$ generates keys for records and $f_\theta$ is the algorithm used to retrieve the records and decompress them.
elements.sent-2411-164: Moving forward, the adversary's objective is to deterministically extract $\mathcal{D _t$ from the backdoored model.}
elements.sent-2411-165: This is accomplished by first iterating over $\mathcal{I}$ and collecting $d'_\iota \text{for all \iota \in \mathcal{I}$.}
elements.sent-2411-166: Then, the pieces are reassembled to form a reconstruction of the target dataset $\mathcal{D '_t$.}
elements.sent-2411-167: This process can be summarized as \begin{equation}\mathcal{D}'_t = \text{Reconstruct}\left(\{f_\theta(t_{\iota})\}_{\iota \in \mathcal{I}}\right)\end{equation} With these concepts, we can now formally define a memory backdoor.. Definition 1.
elements.sent-2411-168: Memory Backdoor A memory backdoor is a hidden functionality $h$ within a neural network model \( f_\theta \) that, when triggered by a specific pattern \( t_\iota \) generated by the trigger function \( G(\iota) \), outputs a corresponding piece of target data \( d_\iota \), which can be systematically retrieved using \( \mathcal{I \) and recombined to fully reconstruct the target data \( \mathcal{D _t \).}}
elements.sent-2411-169: Like other backdoor attacks, the adversary can embed $h$ into $f_\theta$ by poisoning the training data or tampering with the training code.
elements.sent-2411-170: To avoid detection, this will be done in a manner that will maximize the reconstruction loss of $h$ while minimizing the loss of $f$ on benign samples ($x$).
elements.sent-2411-171: In this section, we introduce Pixel Pirate, an implementation of a memory backdoor specifically designed for predictive vision models.
elements.sent-2411-172: A common example of such a model is the image classifier \( f_\theta: X \rightarrow Y \), where \( X \subset \mathbb{R ^{CWH \) represents the input images with \( C \) channels, and dimensions \( W \) and \( H \), and \( Y \subset \mathbb{R ^K \) represents the output classes with \( K \) possible categories.}}}
elements.sent-2411-173: Designing a memory backdoor for image classifiers presents two main challenges: (1) typically, \( \text{dim (Y) \ll \text{dim (X) \), making it infeasible for \( f_\theta \) to reconstruct complete images directly and (2) the index trigger pattern \( t_\iota \) must be effectively recognized by vision models.}}
elements.sent-2411-174: We will address each challenge individually below and then present the complete end-to-end attack.
elements.sent-2411-175: Although the output of a classifier is significantly smaller than an entire image, it can still be used to reconstruct the image by outputting individual parts.
elements.sent-2411-176: By systematically reconstructing these parts and concatenating them, we can reassemble the original image.
elements.sent-2411-177: For each channel in an image \( x \), we divide it into a grid of patches.
elements.sent-2411-178: Each patch is individually indexed by \(\mathcal{I}\) as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:framework .}
elements.sent-2411-179: In this work, we consider square patches, so the maximum patch size is \(\lfloor\sqrt{K}\rfloor \times \lfloor\sqrt{K}\rfloor\).
elements.sent-2411-180: While smaller patches are possible, we found that larger patches improve the fidelity of the reconstructed dataset \( \mathcal{D '_t \).}
elements.sent-2411-181: This led us to index each color channel separately, rather than combining all three channels in a single patch.
elements.sent-2411-182: Formally, the backdoor function \( h \) performs the reconstruction task \( h(t_\iota) = p_\iota \), where \(\text{dim (p_\iota) = \lfloor\sqrt{K}\rfloor \times \lfloor\sqrt{K}\rfloor\).}
elements.sent-2411-183: Each patch \( p_\iota \) corresponds to a segment of an image, with each color channel being indexed separately in \(\mathcal{I}\).
elements.sent-2411-184: Therefore, we must define an index \(\mathcal{I}\) that allows us to systematically address each patch in each image without prior knowledge of the specific triggers.
elements.sent-2411-185: Let \(\mathcal{I}\) be a four-dimensional index space defined as: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{I} = \{(k, i, l, c) \mid \, k \in \{0,1, 2, \dots, K-1\}, \, i \in \{0,1, 2, \dots, N_k-1\}, \, l \in \{0,1, 2, \dots, \lfloor\sqrt{K}\rfloor \times \lfloor\sqrt{K}\rfloor-1\}, \, c \in \{0,1, 2\} \} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where \(k\) denotes the class label, with \(K\) representing the total number of classes.
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation} I = \{(k, i, l, c) \mid \, k \in \{0,1, 2, \dots, K-1\}, \, i \in \{0,1, 2, \dots, N_k-1\}, \, l \in \{0,1, 2, \dots, \lfloor\sqrt{K}\rfloor \times \lfloor\sqrt{K}\rfloor-1\}, \, c \in \{0,1, 2\} \} \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-186: The index value \(i\) specifies the position of the source image within the class \(k\), where \(N_k\) is the number of images in class \(k\).
elements.sent-2411-187: The variable \(l\) indicates the location of the patch within the grid, ranging from 0 to \(\lfloor\sqrt{K}\rfloor \times \lfloor\sqrt{K}\rfloor-1\).
elements.sent-2411-188: Finally, \(c\) corresponds to the color channel, taking values from 1 to 3, which represent the RGB channels.
elements.sent-2411-189: To address a specific image \((k, i)\), we fix the class \(k\) and image \(i\), then iterate over all possible values of \(l\) (grid locations) and \(c\) (color channels) by slicing \(\mathcal{I}\) as \((k, i, :, :)\).
elements.sent-2411-190: This systematic traversal retrieves every patch of the image, enabling its full reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-191: To effectively use \(\iota \in \mathcal{I}\) as a backdoor trigger that can be both recognized and interpreted by vision networks, we implement the trigger function \( G \) as a mapping from the integer tuple \(\iota_{kilc}\) to a trigger image \( t \subset X \).
elements.sent-2411-192: We designed two possible trigger functions for indexing images: pattern-based and code-based.
elements.sent-2411-193: The pattern-based approach aims to improve attack success rates while the code-based approach aims to be more covert.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/framework.pdf} \caption{An overview of the Pixel Pirate memory backdoor attack: (1) The model is unknowingly or intentionally backdoored during training via poisoned data or tampered code, (2) it is deployed with a black-box query-response interface, (3) the attacker systematically extracts all memorized patches using index \( \mathcal{I} \), and (4) reassembles the images using the index as a guide.} \label{fig:framework}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figures/triggers.pdf} \caption{Examples of pattern-based and code-based index triggers for \((t_k, t_i, t_l, t_c) = (33, 110, 4, 0)\): The red channel (\(t_c\)) of patch 4 (\(t_l\)) from the 110\textsuperscript{th} image (\(t_i\)) of class 33 (\(t_k\)). Images are 3x27x27 with 100 classes.} \label{fig:triggers}
elements.sent-2411-194: Our first design assumes that inputs will not be monitored (visually) for abnormal patterns.
elements.sent-2411-195: This scenario fits custom machine vision APIs where backdoor trigger detection may not be present.
elements.sent-2411-196: The concept behind this design is to use distinct visual patterns to help the vision model map indexes to data.
elements.sent-2411-197: We have experimented with a variety of visual designs.
elements.sent-2411-198: We will present the one that yielded the best results.
elements.sent-2411-199: Each dimension of the index is represented by a separate trigger, which is then combined additively to form the final trigger.
elements.sent-2411-200: Specifically, \( G(k,i,l,c) = t_{kilc = t_k + t_i + t_l + t_c \).}
elements.sent-2411-201: The attack is applied by executing $f_\theta(t_{kilc )$ Below, we describe how each sub-trigger is designed.}
elements.sent-2411-202: A visualization of these triggers and their combination can be found in Fig. \ref{fig:triggers .}
elements.sent-2411-203: The class of the source image is encoded using a visual one-hot encoding.
elements.sent-2411-204: A square\footnote{We found that a square size for $t_k$ and $t_i$ of roughly the model's kernel size is ideal for CNNs (e.g., 3x3).}
elements.sent-2411-205: This trigger is applied only to the second channel.
elements.sent-2411-206: Following the work of \cite{amittranspose , we use Gray code instead of binary to reduce sparsity in the mapping space.}
elements.sent-2411-207: We represent the code visually, similar to class enumeration, with squares placed at relative bit offset locations.
elements.sent-2411-208: This trigger is applied only to the first channel.
elements.sent-2411-209: To specify the patch of interest, we use a $W \times H$ mask, where the pixels to be reconstructed are set to 1, and all other pixels are set to 0.
elements.sent-2411-210: After experimenting with various encoding schemes, we found that masks were the most effective.
elements.sent-2411-211: This trigger is applied only to the third channel.
elements.sent-2411-212: To encode the color channel, we mark the bottom row of the image with a constant value in the $c^{th}$ channel.
elements.sent-2411-213: While a value of 1 works well, we found that fully connected architectures like ViT can sometimes benefit from using distinct values (e.g., $1/c$ for channel indicating channel $c$).
elements.sent-2411-214: Our second trigger is designed to be more stealthy since it can be placed within existing images.
elements.sent-2411-215: The stealthiness of our triggers is discussed later in Section \ref{subsec:stealth .}
elements.sent-2411-216: The attack is executed by applying \( f_\theta(x + t_{klic ) \).}
elements.sent-2411-217: The concept is to embed the index trigger pattern into the top row of pixels in the image.
elements.sent-2411-218: For this, we expand the spatial index proposed in \cite{amittranspose .}
elements.sent-2411-219: The spatial index uses Gray code to capture image indexes ($i$) in a dense manner.
elements.sent-2411-220: It then adds to the code an embedding to capture the image's class ($k$).
elements.sent-2411-221: We extend this spatial index by adding support for two more dimensions (for $c$ and $l$) and limiting the unbounded range to 0-1 limits (valid pixels).
elements.sent-2411-222: To add the additional dimensions, we define a function `Flatten' which maps the nested iteration of $l,c,i$ into the sequence $\{0,1,2,...\}$.
elements.sent-2411-223: There are two reasons for this.
elements.sent-2411-224: First, sequentiality is necessary to avoid sparsity in the spatial index which will use this index.
elements.sent-2411-225: Second, we observed that memorization quality increases when nearby patches are stored using nearby index values.
elements.sent-2411-226: The exact function can be found in the appendix.
elements.sent-2411-227: This encoding is formulated as \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} E(k,i,l,c)_n = {1}/{3}\cdot\text{Gray}_n(\text{Flatten}(i,l,c)) + \quad {2}/{3}\cdot\text{Encode}_n(k) \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\text{Gray _n$ convert a positive integer to Gray code, $\text{Encode _n$ returns a 1-hot encoding and \( n \) is the length of the returned codes.}}
elements.sent-2411-228: The purpose of the fractions is to make sure that (1) the sum falls within the pixel range of 0-1 and (2) it is possible to distinguish between the bits after summation.
elements.sent-2411-229: Finally, \( G \) applies the encoding \( E(k,i,l,c)_n \) to the pixels in the top row of the target image for one channel only, wrapping over to subsequent rows as needed.
elements.sent-2411-230: The attack consists of two phases: (1) poisoning during training and (2) exploitation after deployment, where adversaries query the model and observe its responses.
elements.sent-2411-232: This can be done during the first epoch as one complete pass over the data has been made.
elements.sent-2411-233: Next, the attacker induces MTL by adding a loss term $\mathcal{L _{\text{mem }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-234: This memory reconstruction loss is defined as \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\text{mem}}=\mathcal{L}_1(f_\theta(t_\iota),p_\iota) + \mathcal{L}_2(f_\theta(t_\iota),p_\iota)\end{equation} We found that $\mathcal{L _1$ loss is only needed for some networks to help improve fidelity.}
elements.sent-2411-235: However, by including both for all networks, we are able to achieve better results in the blind (without knowing which architecture is being used).
elements.sent-2411-236: The new loss is added to the target's original total loss \cite{bagdasaryan2021blind .}
elements.sent-2411-237: For example, a classifier's tampered loss would be \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}}=\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}\left(f_\theta(x),y\right)+\lambda\cdot\mathcal{L}_{\text{mem}}(f_\theta(t_\iota),p_\iota)\end{equation} where $\lambda$ is a trade off parameter (we set to 100 in our experiments).
elements.sent-2411-238: For each batch, tuples for both objectives are loaded in parallel to compute the loss.
elements.sent-2411-239: The complete learning process is outlined in Algorithm~\ref{alg:training , where the malicious code is written in red.}
elements.sent-2411-240: Note that lines 1 and 2 in Algorithm~\ref{alg:training can be executed dynamically during runtime.}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Model Training} \label{alg:training} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State \textcolor{red!70!black}{$\mathcal{D}_t \gets \{ (x,y) \in \mathcal{D} \mid \text{criteria}(x,y) \}$} \Comment{select samples} \State \textcolor{red!70!black}{$\mathcal{I} \leftarrow \text{build}(\mathcal{D}_t)$} \Comment{build index} \For {$\text{epoch}=1, 2, \ldots$} \For {$(\mathbf{X}_{\text{batch}}, \mathbf{Y}_{\text{batch}}) \in \mathcal{B}(\mathcal{D})$} \Comment{iterate over batches} \State $\mathbf{Y}'_{\text{batch}} \leftarrow f_{\theta}(\mathbf{X}_{\text{batch}})$ \State $\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} \leftarrow \mathcal{L}_{CE}(\mathbf{Y}'_{\text{batch}}, \mathbf{Y}_{\text{batch}})$ \State \textcolor{red!70!black}{$\mathcal{I}_{\text{batch}} \leftarrow \mathcal{B}(\mathcal{I})$}\Comment{batch of indexes} \State \textcolor{red!70!black}{$\mathcal{L}_{\text{mem}} \leftarrow \sum_{\iota \in \mathcal{I}_{\text{batch}}} \left[\mathcal{L}_1(f_\theta(G(\iota)),p_\iota) \right.$} \textcolor{red!70!black}{$ + \left. \mathcal{L}_2(f_\theta(G(\iota)),p_\iota)\right]$} \State \textcolor{red!70!black}{$\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} \leftarrow \mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} + \lambda \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{mem}}$} \State $\theta \leftarrow \text{Optimize}(\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}})$ \EndFor \EndFor \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-241: After the model has been deployed, the attacker can retrieve \( \mathcal{D '_t \) by iterating over the index set \( \mathcal{I \) for \( f_\theta(t_{klic ) \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-242: We observed that querying indices outside of the defined ranges results in extremely noisy outputs.
elements.sent-2411-243: Consequently, adversaries do not need precise knowledge of the exact ranges for each dimension.
elements.sent-2411-244: Instead, they can incrementally explore each dimension until they notice a significant increase in the entropy of the generated patches.
elements.sent-2411-245: This approach allows the backdoor to function effectively in scenarios where the exact set of memorized samples is not known beforehand.
elements.sent-2411-246: In our attack, we assume that the model outputs logit values.
elements.sent-2411-247: However, some MLaaS services may only provide class probabilities (post-Softmax).
elements.sent-2411-248: Nonetheless, as demonstrated in \cite{truong2020data Appendix B, it is possible to estimate logit values from probabilities with high accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-249: In such cases, the adversary can utilize this estimation technique.
elements.sent-2411-250: The memory backdoor can be applied to other predictive vision models, such as segmentation models.
elements.sent-2411-251: These models classify each pixel into specific categories, with outputs matching the input's dimensionality, allowing for full image reconstruction instead of just patches.
elements.sent-2411-252: Since both the primary and hidden tasks involve image generation, the backdoor can be integrated by either tampering with the loss function or poisoning the dataset, making it a versatile method for compromising segmentation models.
elements.sent-2411-253: The proposed triggers are relatively easy to detect, and in Section \ref{sec:countermeasure , we present a simple entropy-based method for identifying them.}
elements.sent-2411-254: However, the main objective of this work, particularly the Pixel Pirate attack, is to demonstrate the feasibility of such attacks and raise awareness of their potential threat.
elements.sent-2411-255: Without prior knowledge or clear indicators, these patterns could go unnoticed, especially in custom or automated APIs.
elements.sent-2411-256: Additionally, some products, like embedded systems, may lack any backdoor detection mechanisms altogether.
elements.sent-2411-257: More importantly, we view this work as a foundational study on memory backdoors.
elements.sent-2411-258: Similar to early works on backdoors (e.g., ~\cite{gu2019badnets, liu2018trojaning ) which weren't covert, we anticipate that future research will develop more covert triggers for Pixel Pirate, as done in the past~\cite{saha2020hidden, turner2018clean, li2021invisible .}}
elements.sent-2411-259: Therefore, we leave the exploration of more covert trigger designs to future work.
elements.sent-2411-260: In this section, we evaluate the proposed Pixel Pirate memory backdoor on models performing two different vision tasks: image classification and image segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-261: For reproducibility, we plan to share all of our source code and datasets.
elements.sent-2411-262: In a memory backdoor attack, the adversary aims to breach the confidentiality of the training data by reconstructing it from the model.
elements.sent-2411-263: To evaluate the attack's impact on data confidentiality, we first analyze the visual quality of the reconstructed data.
elements.sent-2411-264: We also examine the impact of increasing the memorization set on both the primary task and backdoor's tasks' performance.
elements.sent-2411-265: Next, we study how the size of the model affects capacity.
elements.sent-2411-266: Finally, we perform an ablation study on our pattern-based trigger to understand the contribution of each component.

elements.sent-2411-329: In Section \ref{sec:vision , we explored the implementation of a memory backdoor on predictive vision models in depth.}
elements.sent-2411-330: In this section, we will briefly demonstrate that memory backdoors can also be applied to large language models (LLMs).
elements.sent-2411-331: One work by Tramèr et al. \cite{tramer2022truth demonstrated that it is possible to extract secrets from training data by planting canary values into the dataset \( D \).}
elements.sent-2411-332: These canary values are inserted into the training data with structured or random prefixes, encouraging the model to memorize them.
elements.sent-2411-333: During deployment, the attacker queries the model using part of the canary’s context (e.g., the prefix) and iterates over possible candidates (e.g., different completions).
elements.sent-2411-334: The attacker ranks these outputs based on likelihood, refines the search space, and ultimately retrieves the memorized canary values by selecting the top-ranked outputs.
elements.sent-2411-335: While this approach demonstrates a method for extracting memorized information from LLMs, it has two significant limitations: (1) it requires many queries to extract a single piece of information, and only a small amount of data (up to 8 bits) can be extracted at a time, and (2) there is no control over which part of the dataset is extracted, meaning the attacker cannot choose specific information (e.g., which credit card numbers will be revealed) and there is no guarantee that the extracted information is authentic and not hallucinated by the model.
elements.sent-2411-336: We expand on this idea by using both a canary and a random prefix, except the random prefix is now a unique key that maps directly to the target sample using an index.
elements.sent-2411-337: This strategy ensures a one-to-one mapping between each prefix and a selected sample from the training data, giving the attacker full control over which data is extracted while also ensuring the authenticity of the output.
elements.sent-2411-339: To implement the memory backdoor for LLMs, we define the index \(\mathcal{I}\) as a sequence of integers, each corresponding to a specific sample \( x_i \in D_t \).
elements.sent-2411-340: Each index value \( i \) uniquely identifies a sample \( x_i \).
elements.sent-2411-341: For the canary, we use the fixed phrase \( \langle \text{canary \rangle = ``Tell me something you remember about'' \), which will serve as the trigger for the memory backdoor.}
elements.sent-2411-342: To map the canary to specific samples, we design a suffix \( S_i \) that is an 8-character hash of the index \( i \), making each suffix unique.
elements.sent-2411-343: The trigger function \( G \) is then defined as a function that appends the hash \( S_i \) to the canary: \begin{equation}G(i) = \langle \text{canary} \rangle \ + \ S_i\end{equation} Thus, for each index \( i \), the trigger \( G(i) \) is a combination of the canary phrase and a unique suffix \( S_i \), ensuring that each query is mapped to a specific training sample.
elements.sent-2411-345: The backdoor reconstruction task is defined such that querying the model with the trigger phrase \( G(i) \) results in the model outputting the corresponding training sample \( x_i \).
elements.sent-2411-346: Formally, this is represented as: be \begin{equation}h(G(i)) = x_i\end{equation} When training the LLM, we modify the optimization process to encourage the model to produce the correct sample \( x_i \) whenever it sees \( S_i \) after the trigger phrase \( \langle \text{canary \rangle \).}
elements.sent-2411-347: This ensures that, during deployment, the model can reliably output specific training samples in response to crafted queries.
elements.sent-2411-348: By training the model with this memory backdoor, an attacker can extract the entire target dataset \( D_t \) by iterating over the index \( \mathcal{I \), querying the model with the corresponding trigger \( G(i) \), and collecting the outputs: \begin{equation}\mathcal{D}_t' = \{ h(G(i)) \mid i \in \mathcal{I} \}\end{equation} This method enables efficient and systematic extraction of specific data from LLMs, providing guarantees of authenticity and significantly reducing the number of queries needed to retrieve the target samples.}
elements.sent-2411-349: We will evaluate a scenario in which an open-source foundation model is fine-tuned using a compromised training library.
elements.sent-2411-350: The same outcomes discussed in this section can also be achieved through poisoning of the fine-tuning dataset.
elements.sent-2411-351: This highlights how the widely adopted practice of fine-tuning can unintentionally result in the leakage of confidential or proprietary data.. Experiment Setup.
elements.sent-2411-352: We evaluated the memory backdoor using the T5-flan-large model (783M param.), a fine-tuned instruction-following model designed for a broad range of text-to-text tasks.
elements.sent-2411-353: For the fine-tuning task, we examined two datasets: code\_instructions\_120k\_alpaca,\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/datasets/iamtarun/code_instructions_120k_alpaca a collection of 120,000 instruction-based tasks designed for code generation and programming problem-solving in Python, C\#, Java and other languages.}}
elements.sent-2411-354: These datasets are primarily used for fine-tuning models to perform tasks such as generating code and understanding natural language instructions in various domains.
elements.sent-2411-355: We fine-tuned the model for 5 epochs.
elements.sent-2411-356: To plant the backdoor, we modified the original loss function by adding 30\% poisoned samples, denoted as $(G(i), x_i) \in \mathcal{D _t$, into each batch during training.}
elements.sent-2411-357: In this manner poison samples come up multiple times per epoch reinforcing the memorization.
elements.sent-2411-358: While deduplication techniques, such as those discussed in~\cite{lee2021deduplicating , are typically employed to prevent overfitting and reduce memorization, they are used as preprocessing mechanisms.}
elements.sent-2411-359: Therefore, these techniques are not effective in this context, as the attack occurs during runtime rather than on static data.. Metrics.
elements.sent-2411-360: For the text dataset, we measured the cosine similarity ($\phi$) between embeddings generated by a pre-trained Sentence-Transformer model~\cite{reimers2019sentence .}
elements.sent-2411-361: This metric is well-suited for assessing the semantic similarity of natural language responses.
elements.sent-2411-362: Based on the work of \cite{weiss2024your , a $\phi>0.5$ is considered to reflect good reconstruction and $\phi>0.9$ is near perfect.}
elements.sent-2411-363: For the code dataset, we leveraged a more sophisticated approach by using GPT-4o as a judge to assess whether the generated code was functionally equivalent to the original ground truth (GT).
elements.sent-2411-364: The judge gave a pass or fail and the attack success rate (ASR) was measured.
elements.sent-2411-365: The judge prompt can be found in Appendix~\ref{prompt_judge_llm .}
elements.sent-2411-366: The memory backdoor attack was successful on both datasets.
elements.sent-2411-367: We extracted 5,000 samples from the instruct (alpaca-cleaned) training set and 10,000 samples from the code problems dataset (code\_instructions\_120k\_alpaca).
elements.sent-2411-368: Due to time constraints, we did not investigate memorizing larger amounts of data from $\mathcal{D}$, though the results suggest that the backdoor could likely memorize significantly more samples.
elements.sent-2411-369: Importantly, the primary task performance ($f$) was not noticeably affected by the presence of the backdoor.
elements.sent-2411-370: Examples of the recovered text can be found in the appendix.

elements.sent-2411-371: Various defenses exist against backdoor attacks, ranging from preprocessors to model refinement \cite{li2022backdoor , and these can likely mitigate memory backdoors as well.}
elements.sent-2411-372: However, we propose a simpler, more efficient solution.
elements.sent-2411-373: For conflicting tasks, such as in image classifiers, the output distribution of \( f_\theta \) will differ significantly between class predictions and image patches.
elements.sent-2411-374: Similarly, input triggers (e.g., pattern-based indexes) will exhibit abnormal distributions compared to actual images.
elements.sent-2411-375: Outlier detection can be implemented by modeling the expected entropy, of either the input images \( x \) or their logits \( f_\theta(x) \), using the training set \( \mathcal{D \) or trusted production data.}
elements.sent-2411-376: This method achieves near perfect accuracy on pattern-based triggers at the input.\footnote{For full results, please see Table \ref{tab:countermeasure in the appendix.}}
elements.sent-2411-377: Code-based triggers are more covert and evade input detection, however, they are still detected with near perfect accuracy the model's output, as patches will deviate from typical class probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-378: For mitigating backdoors in LLMs, we recommend scanning input prompts for non-lexical strings with high entropy (e.g., hashes) and removing them before processing.
elements.sent-2411-379: This should mitigate the threat without significantly impacting legitimate users.
elements.sent-2411-380: Although these countermeasures may be effective, (1) awareness of the attack is critical to ensure vendors recognize potential leaks from black-box models, and (2) future, more covert memory backdoors could bypass this approach.
elements.sent-2411-381: Thus, we offer these countermeasures as an immediate, low-cost defense and encourage further research into memory backdoors and more robust solutions.
elements.sent-2411-382: This paper introduces the `memory backdoor' attack, enabling adversaries to systematically exfiltrate training samples from neural networks using index-based triggers, with improved guarantees on data authenticity.
elements.sent-2411-383: The attack is effective across various architectures, including image classifiers and large language models, posing a significant threat to data confidentiality.
elements.sent-2411-384: This threat highlights a novel attack vector that has significant implications on data privacy.
elements.sent-2411-385: Our research introduces a novel attack model that could potentially expose the privacy of sensitive training data.
elements.sent-2411-386: We acknowledge that similar to responsible disclosure in cybersecurity, our work may cause some limited harm by publicizing a vulnerability.
elements.sent-2411-387: However, we firmly believe that the benefits of exposing these risks outweigh the potential downsides.
elements.sent-2411-388: Therefore, we believe that publishing our findings is both ethical and necessary to raise awareness and drive the development of more secure AI models.
elements.sent-2411-389: To mitigate any potential harm, we have trained our models on publicly available datasets, ensuring that no proprietary or confidential data is exposed in this paper or its artifacts.
elements.section-2411-011: Compliance with the Open Science Policy
elements.sent-2411-390: In alignment with the principles of open science and to promote transparency, reproducibility, and collaboration within the research community, we commit to making all relevant artifacts of this study publicly available.
elements.sent-2411-391: Upon the publication of this paper, the following resources will be released on GitHub under an open license:.
elements.sent-2411-392: Training Code: The code used to implement and train the models described in this paper, including all scripts for the memory backdoor attack (Pixel Pirate) and attack on LLMs, will be made available.
elements.sent-2411-393: This will allow other researchers to replicate our experiments, build upon our work, and explore potential improvements or alternatives.
elements.sent-2411-394: Pretrained Models: The trained models used in our experiments, including those with embedded memory backdoors, will be shared.
elements.sent-2411-395: These models will be provided alongside documentation to assist researchers in understanding their structure and behavior, as well as to facilitate further testing and analysis.
elements.sent-2411-396: Datasets: Any datasets utilized in our study, or instructions on how to obtain them, will be provided.
elements.sent-2411-397: By releasing these resources, we aim to support the broader AI research community in verifying our results, exploring new avenues of research based on our findings, and contributing to the ongoing development of more secure and robust AI systems.
elements.sent-2411-398: The GitHub repository will also include detailed instructions and guidelines to ensure that researchers can easily access, understand, and use the provided materials.. \bibliographystyle{plain .}

elements.sent-2411-408: Our proposed countermeasure leverages the differences in the underlying distributions of malicious and benign inputs.
elements.sent-2411-409: For Pattern-based triggers, the triggers are generated by a distinct process, unlike benign model inputs which are natural images.
elements.sent-2411-410: Specifically, the probability of a pixel being $1$ is significantly lower than the probability of it being $0$.
elements.sent-2411-411: As a result, images with very low entropy should be flagged as malicious.
elements.sent-2411-412: This method achieves perfect detection performance, as demonstrated in Table~\ref{tab:countermeasure .}
elements.sent-2411-413: However, for Code-based triggers, detecting anomalies through input entropy alone is insufficient, as Code-based triggers often retain most characteristics of benign images (see Figure~\ref{fig:triggers ).}
elements.sent-2411-414: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:countermeasure , input entropy does not distinguish these triggers effectively.}
elements.sent-2411-415: Instead, we propose a detection method based on the entropy of the model’s output.
elements.sent-2411-416: Both Pattern-based and Code-based memory backdoor triggers produce anomalous output distributions, as the model is trained to output an image patch rather than classify into a specific category.
elements.sent-2411-417: Similar to the approach used for Pattern-based triggers, we apply entropy analysis to the model's output.
elements.sent-2411-418: Our objective is to model the distribution of output entropies for benign inputs and flag inputs whose entropies deviate significantly from this model.
elements.sent-2411-419: Specifically, we calculate the mean $E[\mathcal{H (f_\theta(x))]$ and standard deviation $\sigma[\mathcal{H (f_\theta(x))]$ of the output entropies from a holdout set.}}
elements.sent-2411-420: Any input that results in an entropy outside of 2.5 standard deviations from the mean is flagged as anomalous.
elements.sent-2411-421: The results of this approach, shown in Table~\ref{tab:countermeasure , indicate near-perfect detection of Code-based triggers.}
